                - paragraph [ref=e374]:
                  - strong [ref=e375]:
                    - text: You are a victim of
                    - link "branch prediction" [ref=e376] [cursor=pointer]:
                      - /url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Branch_predictor
                    - text: fail.
                - separator [ref=e377]
                - heading "What is Branch Prediction?" [level=2] [ref=e378]
                - paragraph [ref=e379]: "Consider a railroad junction:"
                - paragraph [ref=e380]:
                  - link "Image showing a railroad junction" [ref=e381] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Entroncamento_do_Transpraia.JPG
                    - img "Image showing a railroad junction" [ref=e382]
                  - subscript [ref=e383]:
                    - link "Image" [ref=e384] [cursor=pointer]:
                      - /url: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Entroncamento_do_Transpraia.JPG
                    - text: by Mecanismo, via Wikimedia Commons. Used under the
                    - link "CC-By-SA 3.0" [ref=e385] [cursor=pointer]:
                      - /url: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/3.0/deed.en
                    - text: license.
                - paragraph [ref=e386]: Now for the sake of argument, suppose this is back in the 1800s - before long-distance or radio communication.
                - paragraph [ref=e387]: You are a blind operator of a junction and you hear a train coming. You have no idea which way it is supposed to go. You stop the train to ask the driver which direction they want. And then you set the switch appropriately.
                - paragraph [ref=e388]:
                  - emphasis [ref=e389]: Trains are heavy and have a lot of inertia, so they take forever to start up and slow down.
                - paragraph [ref=e390]: Is there a better way? You guess which direction the train will go!
                - list [ref=e391]:
                  - listitem [ref=e392]: If you guessed right, it continues on.
                  - listitem [ref=e393]: If you guessed wrong, the driver will stop, back up, and yell at you to flip the switch. Then it can restart down the other path.
                - paragraph [ref=e394]:
                  - strong [ref=e395]: If you guess right every time
                  - text: ", the train will never have to stop."
                  - strong [ref=e396]: If you guess wrong too often
                  - text: ", the train will spend a lot of time stopping, backing up, and restarting."
                - separator [ref=e397]
                - paragraph [ref=e398]:
                  - strong [ref=e399]: "Consider an if-statement:"
                  - text: "At the processor level, it is a branch instruction:"
                - paragraph [ref=e400]:
                  - img "if(x >= 128) compiles into a jump-if-less-than processor instruction." [ref=e401]
                - paragraph [ref=e402]: You are a processor and you see a branch. You have no idea which way it will go. What do you do? You halt execution and wait until the previous instructions are complete. Then you continue down the correct path.
                - paragraph [ref=e403]:
                  - emphasis [ref=e404]: Modern processors are complicated and have long pipelines. This means they take forever to "warm up" and "slow down".
                - paragraph [ref=e405]: Is there a better way? You guess which direction the branch will go!
                - list [ref=e406]:
                  - listitem [ref=e407]: If you guessed right, you continue executing.
                  - listitem [ref=e408]: If you guessed wrong, you need to flush the pipeline and roll back to the branch. Then you can restart down the other path.
                - paragraph [ref=e409]:
                  - strong [ref=e410]: If you guess right every time
                  - text: ", the execution will never have to stop."
                  - strong [ref=e411]: If you guess wrong too often
                  - text: ", you spend a lot of time stalling, rolling back, and restarting."
                - separator [ref=e412]
                - paragraph [ref=e413]: This is branch prediction. I admit it's not the best analogy since the train could just signal the direction with a flag. But in computers, the processor doesn't know which direction a branch will go until the last moment.
                - paragraph [ref=e414]: How would you strategically guess to minimize the number of times that the train must back up and go down the other path? You look at the past history! If the train goes left 99% of the time, then you guess left. If it alternates, then you alternate your guesses. If it goes one way every three times, you guess the same...
                - paragraph [ref=e415]:
                  - emphasis [ref=e416]:
                    - strong [ref=e417]: In other words, you try to identify a pattern and follow it.
                  - text: This is more or less how branch predictors work.
                - paragraph [ref=e418]: Most applications have well-behaved branches. Therefore, modern branch predictors will typically achieve >90% hit rates. But when faced with unpredictable branches with no recognizable patterns, branch predictors are virtually useless.
                - paragraph [ref=e419]:
                  - text: "Further reading:"
                  - link "\"Branch predictor\" article on Wikipedia" [ref=e420] [cursor=pointer]:
                    - /url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Branch_predictor
                  - text: .
                - separator [ref=e421]
                - heading "As hinted from above, the culprit is this if-statement:" [level=2] [ref=e422]

                - paragraph [ref=e432]: Notice that the data is evenly distributed between 0 and 255. When the data is sorted, roughly the first half of the iterations will not enter the if-statement. After that, they will all enter the if-statement.
                - paragraph [ref=e433]: This is very friendly to the branch predictor since the branch consecutively goes the same direction many times. Even a simple saturating counter will correctly predict the branch except for the few iterations after it switches direction.
                - paragraph [ref=e434]:
                  - strong [ref=e435]: "Quick visualization:"
                - code [ref=e437]: T = branch taken N = branch not taken data[] = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, ... 126, 127, 128, 129, 130, ... 250, 251, 252, ... branch = N N N N N ... N N T T T ... T T T ... = NNNNNNNNNNNN ... NNNNNNNTTTTTTTTT ... TTTTTTTTTT (easy to predict)
                - paragraph [ref=e438]: However, when the data is completely random, the branch predictor is rendered useless, because it can't predict random data. Thus there will probably be around 50% misprediction (no better than random guessing).
                - code [ref=e440]: data[] = 226, 185, 125, 158, 198, 144, 217, 79, 202, 118, 14, 150, 177, 182, ... branch = T, T, N, T, T, T, T, N, T, N, N, T, T, T ... = TTNTTTTNTNNTTT ... (completely random - impossible to predict)
                - separator [ref=e441]

                - paragraph [ref=e518]: A general rule of thumb is to avoid data-dependent branching in critical loops (such as in this example).
                - separator [ref=e519]

                  - listitem [ref=e523]:

                      - text: GCC 4.6.1 with

                      - text: on x64 is able to generate a conditional move, so there is no difference between the sorted and unsorted data - both are fast. This is called "if-conversion" (to branchless) and is necessary for vectorization but also sometimes good for scalar.
                    - paragraph [ref=e527]:

                      - code [ref=e528]: cmov

                      - link "interchanges the two loops" [ref=e539] [cursor=pointer]:
                        - /url: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Loop_interchange
                      - text: ", thereby hoisting the unpredictable branch to the outer loop. Not only is it immune to the mispredictions, it's also twice as fast as whatever VC++ and GCC can generate! In other words, ICC took advantage of the test-loop to defeat the benchmark..."
                  - listitem [ref=e540]:

                    - generic [ref=e710]:
                      - generic [ref=e711]: "@Mycotina, I'm no expert, but what I understand is: the processor needs multiple steps to execute a single instruction (fetching, decoding, etc) -- this is called \"instruction pipelining\" -- so, as an optimization, it will fetch multiple instructions at once and \"warm up\" the next instructions while executing the current one. If the wrong branch is chosen, the instructions being \"warmed up\" in the pipeline must be discarded, so that the instructions on the right branch can be put into the pipeline instead."

                      - generic [ref=e748]: "@Mycotina It's easier to understand when you think of the instruction pipeline cache as tracks, the train (with cars) as the instructions, and the indicator of whether you go left or right by some dude at the END of the train; not the beginning. By the time you see him to know you've guessed right, not only is it too late to switch things, the pipeline ahead is already populated, but in the wrong direction. If you guessed wrong the predicted pipeline needs to be thrown out (derail the train; drag it back before the switch house, put it back on the tracks, and send it the other way)."
